So the farmer has to take them one by one, but he has to make sure that when he leaves something on one side, the others aren't left alone together.

So the key is to figure out the order in which the farmer can transport the items so that no two dangerous items are left together. Let me think step by step.

First, the farmer is on the starting side, let's say the left bank, and needs to get to the right bank. The boat can carry him and one item. So he can take one item across, leave it there, and come back. But he has to do this multiple times.

Okay, let me try to figure this out. So the farmer has a fox, a chicken, and a bag of grain. He needs to cross a river, and his boat can only carry him and one item at a time. The problem is that if left alone, the fox will eat the chicken, and the chicken will eat the grain. So the question is, how can he get everything across safely?

Alright, so the key here is that the farmer can only take one item with him each time. So he can take the fox, or the chicken, or the grain, but not more than one. And when he leaves something on the other side, he can't take it back unless he goes back himself. But the problem is that if the fox and chicken are left alone together, the fox will eat the chicken. Similarly, if the chicken and grain are left together, the chicken will eat the grain. So the challenge is to transport all three items across without any of them being eaten.

Let me think step by step. Let's consider the possible order of crossings.

First, the farmer is on the starting side with all three items. He needs to take one item across. Let's consider the possibilities:
